danger_signals.visual_form: Unique enemy sprites at end of each round, distinct from standard enemies
danger_signals.meaning: Major threat requiring skill and power-ups to overcome; marks progression checkpoints

danger_signals.visual_form: Final antagonist; implied dark/magical visual identity as evil sorcerer
danger_signals.meaning: Primary danger; represents kidnapping threat and game's central conflict

safety_signals.visual_form: Golden Bell and Silver Bell items on screen
safety_signals.meaning: Temporary offense enhancement; Golden Bell enables projectile attacks, Silver Bell creates protective force field
Core safe offensive capability; hereditary weapon from ninja school tradition
Safe origin point; represents tradition, training, institutional safety

faction_indicators.color_identity: Yellow keikogi (arcade version) / Red keikogi (NES/home ports)
faction_indicators.symbol_or_sigil: Spinning Sword (School of Chirin heritage weapon); punk-rock/radical ninja styling
faction_indicators.environmental_markers: Ninja School setting as home base; feudal Japan aesthetic

faction_indicators.enemy_visual_variants: Final boss; uses kidnapping as power display
faction_indicators.environmental_markers: Destination/end-game location where Princess Margo is held

faction_indicators.symbol_or_sigil: Kidnapping victim; romantic motivation for protagonist quest
faction_indicators.environmental_markers: Off-screen presence; communicated via note attached to struck bird messenger

world_state_to_visual.behavioral_manifestation: Protagonist in stationary training state before disruption

world_state_to_visual.visual_manifestation: Dead bird with attached note falls at Niki's feet; dramatic message delivery
world_state_to_visual.behavioral_manifestation: Niki bursts through school wall; immediate action response; abandons training for rescue quest

world_state_to_visual.visual_manifestation: Seven distinct level rounds with escalating boss encounters
world_state_to_visual.behavioral_manifestation: Continuous forward movement through levels; combat against enemies; boss battles at round ends

world_state_to_visual.visual_manifestation: Golden Bell and Silver Bell items appear during levels
world_state_to_visual.behavioral_manifestation: Temporary combat enhancement; limited-duration visual/mechanical changes to sword behavior

world_state_to_visual.visual_manifestation: Japanese ninja aesthetic; keikogi clothing; traditional school architecture; topknot/chonmage styling (Japan version)
world_state_to_visual.behavioral_manifestation: Ninja-appropriate combat mechanics; hereditary weapon tradition

environmental_storytelling.example: Dead bird with attached note communicates Princess Margo's kidnapping; establishes motivation without cutscene
Keikogi color (yellow arcade vs. red home ports) and radical hair styling communicate ninja school affiliation and 'radical' personality archetype
Spinning Sword described as 'passed down from generation to generation from the School of Chirin' establishes tradition and legitimacy
Beginning at Ninja School establishes protagonist's trained status; wall-breaking exit dramatizes urgency of rescue quest
Seven distinct rounds with boss at each end shows escalating challenge and journey toward Stone Wizard confrontation

entity_behavioral_tells.behavior: Stationed at Ninja School; inactive state
Bursts through wall; immediate directional action; enters quest mode
Uses spinning sword; collects power-ups; engages enemies; progresses through levels
Appear throughout levels; require sword combat or power-up enhanced attacks
Distinct AI; heightened challenge; require accumulated power-ups or skill to defeat
Holds Princess Margo; represents end-game challenge; final boss confrontation

notes: Kid Niki communicates world-state primarily through simple visual markers: costume color/styling for faction identity, power-up item appearance for mechanical state changes, and boss presence for progression checkpoints. The game uses minimal narrative exposition—the kidnapping is communicated via environmental messenger (dead bird) rather than dialogue. Feudal Japan setting and ninja aesthetic are consistent environmental storytelling elements. Two regional variants (arcade yellow keikogi vs. home red; punk spikes vs. chonmage topknot) show localization choices reflecting different market identities for the same mechanical game. Danger is conveyed through enemy sprites and escalating boss difficulty; safety through power-ups and the hereditary weapon's legitimacy narrative.
